description: The provided image displays a publisher 'Check for updates' CrossMark icon rather than a scientific data figure. No sub-panels, micrographs, spectra, diffraction patterns, or data curves are visible within the image pixels. The caption field is empty and no citation sentences are provided. Consequently, no material system, sample designation, processing condition, phase constitution, microstructural feature, or performance metric can be visually extracted from this figure. The accompanying structured text extraction summary contains extensive experimental information about Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C low-density steels alloyed with 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu, including SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, electrochemical impedance, potentiodynamic polarization, and immersion corrosion data referenced to various figures throughout the paper. However, because the visual content lacks any scientific illustration, the specific figure identity—whether it pertains to corrosion morphology, weight loss kinetics, XPS compositional analysis, or electrochemical curves—cannot be determined from the image pixels alone.

Figure 1 combines scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–xCu low-density steels containing 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu. Subfigures (a)–(c) display the surface microstructures of the 0Cu, 1Cu, and 3Cu alloys, respectively, all exhibiting equiaxed austenite grains with visible annealing twins and a 10 μm scale bar. In the 0Cu alloy, sparse white particles adhere to austenite grain surfaces. With 1 wt% Cu, numerous fine white particles populate the grain surfaces, with a minority locating at grain boundaries. In the 3Cu alloy, white granular objects are markedly enriched along austenite grain boundaries, while fewer remain on grain surfaces. Subfigure (d) presents XRD patterns for all three compositions, showing distinct γ-austenite diffraction peaks indexed as (111), (200), (220), and (311), with no additional phases detected. The data collectively establish that Cu addition progressively redistributes precipitated particles from grain surfaces toward grain boundaries while the matrix retains a single-phase austenitic structure, providing the microstructural basis for interpreting corrosion behavior.

Figure 2 presents grain-size statistics for Fe–30Mn–8Al–C–xCu low-density steels containing 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu. Panels (a)–(c) display optical-microscopy-based austenite grain maps in which grain boundaries were identified and pseudo-colored via grey-segmentation image processing (MPAR software); each panel includes a 20 μm scale bar and reports an ASTM grain size number determined by the random-lines method. The companion histograms plot the equivalent-diameter distributions for the corresponding samples. The 0Cu sample (a) shows the finest microstructure, with an ASTM grain size grade of 10.2 and a histogram peak near 5–10 μm. The 1Cu sample (b) exhibits noticeable coarsening to ASTM 9.5, with the distribution shifting toward 10–15 μm, while the 3Cu sample (c) has an ASTM grade of 9.6 and a broader size distribution extending to larger diameters. Collectively, the data demonstrate that Cu addition promotes austenite grain nucleation and growth during thermomechanical processing, yielding significantly larger average grain sizes in the Cu-containing steels relative to the Cu-free base alloy.

This figure displays SEM micrographs and EPMA elemental mapping for Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C steel containing 1 wt% Cu (a) and 3 wt% Cu (b), illustrating the microstructural role of Cu addition. Panel (a) shows secondary electron images with corresponding Al, Nb, Cu, C, and Mo maps for 1Cu: Nb and C co-segregate at white particles attached to the austenite matrix, consistent with NbC-type carbides, while Cu exhibits slight grain-boundary enrichment. Panel (b) presents the same suite of maps for 3Cu, revealing pronounced Cu enrichment along austenite grain boundaries and uneven Al distribution at certain boundaries; here, grain-boundary white particles are identified as elemental Cu, whereas matrix-surface particles correspond to residual NbC carbides. The figure demonstrates that increasing Cu content transforms the precipitate population from NbC-dominated surface carbides to substantial Cu grain-boundary segregation, providing compositional evidence for the microgalvanic corrosion sites described in the study.

This figure presents the statistical analysis of carbide number and equivalent diameter in Fe–30Mn–8Al–C–xCu low-density steels containing 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu. Panels (a–c) show SEM-based micrographs of the austenite grain structure with carbides highlighted via MPAR software. For the 0Cu alloy (a), the average carbide size is 0.52 μm with a total particle count of 1740. The 1Cu alloy (b) exhibits a refined average size of 0.51 μm and a sharply increased carbide number of 2377, indicating a denser distribution on austenite grain surfaces. In contrast, the 3Cu alloy (c) shows a drastic reduction in carbide count to 876 and an increased mean size of approximately 0.61 μm. The accompanying histograms display the equivalent diameter distributions, where the peak shifts from ~0.45 μm for 0Cu, to ~0.40 μm for 1Cu, and rightward to ~0.6 μm for 3Cu, with a markedly lower overall count in the 3Cu sample. These trends demonstrate that 1 wt% Cu refines and increases carbide density, whereas 3 wt% Cu hinders carbon diffusion, leading to carbide coarsening and a sharp decline in particle number.

Figure 5 displays the three-electrode potentiodynamic polarization curves for Fe–30Mn–8Al–C–xCu low-density steels containing 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu (denoted 0 Cu, 1 Cu, and 3 Cu) measured in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature, plotting potential (E vs SCE) against the logarithm of current density (log i in A/cm²). The 0 Cu curve (black squares) shows the widest passive plateau and the noblest breakdown potential, whereas the 3 Cu curve (blue triangles) exhibits the narrowest passive region and the most negative pitting potential, signaling the highest localized corrosion sensitivity. The 1 Cu curve (red circles) lies intermediately. Additionally, the cathodic branch current density visibly increases with Cu content. These observations support the conclusion that Cu alloying progressively impairs the corrosion resistance of the austenite matrix, primarily due to microgalvanic coupling driven by Cu segregation at grain boundaries—most pronounced in the 3 Cu steel—consistent with the reported corrosion rates and EIS data.

Figure 7 presents Mott–Schottky curves (C⁻² versus potential, V_SCE) for Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–xCu low-density steels with 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The plot is divided into three regions: Region I (approximately −1.2 to −0.5 V) shows positive slopes indicating n-type semiconductor behavior of the passive film; Region II (−0.5 to −0.2 V) exhibits a peak representing the transition zone; and Region III (> −0.2 V) displays negative slopes characteristic of p-type semiconductor behavior. The curves reveal that increasing Cu content progressively raises the C⁻² values in the linear regions. The caption states that the calculated donor (N_D) and acceptor (N_A) densities increase from 0Cu to 3Cu, indicating a higher concentration of point defects in the passive film with Cu addition. This trend supports the finding that Cu alloying degrades the protective quality of the passive film, consistent with the observed deterioration of corrosion resistance—particularly the severe grain-boundary microgalvanic corrosion in the 3Cu steel.

Figure 6 presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) characterization of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C–xCu low-density steels with 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature. Panel (a) shows Nyquist plots where discrete experimental points (black squares for 0 Cu, red circles for 1 Cu, blue triangles for 3 Cu) and solid fitted lines (Fitting-0/1/3 Cu) reveal capacitive semicircles whose diameters shrink markedly as Cu content increases; the inset depicts the equivalent circuit comprising solution resistance (Rs), a constant phase element (CPE), and charge transfer resistance (Rct). Panel (b) presents Bode plots of impedance modulus |Z| and negative phase angle versus frequency, confirming that the 0 Cu alloy maintains the highest impedance and broadest phase-angle peak, whereas the 3 Cu alloy exhibits the lowest values. The systematic decrease in semicircle diameter and impedance modulus with Cu addition indicates deteriorating corrosion resistance, which the study attributes to enhanced microgalvanic coupling between Cu-enriched austenite grain boundaries and the matrix, corroborated by SKPFM and XPS analyses showing higher Volta potential differences and altered passive film chemistry at elevated Cu levels.

Figure 8 presents optical microscopy images capturing the time-dependent corrosion morphology of Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–xCu low-density steels with 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu after immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 1–5 days. Panels (a₁)–(a₅) for the 0 Cu alloy show discrete pitting corrosion that gradually enlarges but remains relatively localized. Panels (b₁)–(b₅) for the 1 Cu alloy display chains of interconnected pits propagating across the surface, indicating accelerated attack compared to the Cu-free sample. Panels (c₁)–(c₅) for the 3 Cu alloy reveal severe grain boundary corrosion superimposed on pitting, as the austenite grain boundaries become preferentially attacked due to the microgalvanic coupling between Cu-enriched boundaries and carbides. The systematic comparison demonstrates that Cu addition progressively degrades corrosion resistance, shifting the dominant failure mode from isolated pitting in 0 Cu to extensive grain boundary dissolution in 3 Cu. All micrographs are calibrated with a 20 μm scale bar.

This figure presents immersion corrosion test results for Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–xCu low-density steels (designated 0Cu, 1Cu, and 3Cu, corresponding to 0, 1, and 3 wt% Cu) exposed to a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution over 5 days. Panel (a) plots weight loss versus immersion time: the 0Cu steel shows the slowest mass loss (approximately −0.0020 g at day 5), the 1Cu steel exhibits intermediate degradation (approximately −0.0030 g), and the 3Cu steel suffers the most severe attack with nearly linear weight loss reaching about −0.0035 g. Panel (b) displays the corresponding average corrosion rates in mm·a⁻¹: the 0Cu steel has the lowest rate at approximately 0.055 mm/a (the caption reports 0.0546 mm/a), whereas the 1Cu and 3Cu steels show progressively higher rates of roughly 0.081 and 0.092 mm/a, respectively. The data collectively demonstrate that Cu alloying markedly accelerates corrosion, with both cumulative weight loss and corrosion rate increasing monotonically with Cu content, consistent with prior potentiodynamic polarization and EIS analyses. This supports the conclusion that Cu addition deteriorates the corrosion resistance of the austenitic steel matrix via microgalvanic coupling effects.

This figure presents scanning Kelvin probe force microscopy (SKPFM) measurements comparing the surface height and Volta potential distribution of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steel without Cu (0 Cu) and with 3 wt% Cu (3 Cu). Panels (a) and (d) show AFM topographical maps with height scales ranging from −1.9 μm to 802.5 nm for 0 Cu and −375.3 nm to 832.5 nm for 3 Cu, respectively, both with a 4 μm scale bar. Panels (b) and (e) display the corresponding Volta potential maps (ranges: −5.8 to 13 mV for 0 Cu; −9.4 to 18.2 mV for 3 Cu) with red lines indicating scan profiles. Panels (c) and (f) plot the Volta potential line profiles, revealing uneven potential distributions. Caption reports that the potential difference between the austenite matrix and carbide-surrounding area is 0.9 mV in 0 Cu, while 3 Cu exhibits a larger 1.2 mV difference between Cu-enriched grain boundaries and the austenite matrix. These Volta potential differences drive microgalvanic corrosion, indicating preferential anodic dissolution of austenite and accelerated grain boundary attack in the 3 Cu alloy.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM characterization of the 0Cu Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C low-density steel, focusing on the austenitic matrix and carbide precipitates. Panel (a) shows the austenitic matrix morphology. Panel (b) displays selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns from intracrystalline precipitates, with indexed spots including 002, 0̄20, 001, and 01̄1. Panel (c) provides the dark-field image revealing the nanoscale distribution of intracrystalline precipitates. Panel (d) exhibits the morphology of larger carbides within the matrix. Panel (e) combines a high-resolution TEM image of a carbide region with its corresponding SAED pattern along the [001] zone axis. Panel (f) presents an inverse FFT lattice-fringe image near a carbide, highlighting atomic-scale structural details at the carbide–matrix interface. Collectively, these images confirm an FCC austenite matrix containing intragranular κ-carbide precipitates and MC-type carbides (Nb-rich) on the matrix surface, with edge dislocations and lattice distortion near carbides that act as preferential anodic sites during corrosion.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM investigation of the 1Cu (Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–1Cu) austenite matrix and carbides. Subfigure (a) shows the austenitic matrix with visible grain boundaries and dislocation structures at 1 μm scale. Subfigure (b) displays selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of intracrystalline precipitates along the [001] zone axis, with indexed spots including 0ī0, 00ī, 020, and 022, confirming the crystal structure. Subfigure (c) provides the dark-field morphology revealing fine intracrystalline precipitates at the nanoscale (20 nm scale bar). Subfigure (d) shows larger carbide particles within the matrix at 200 nm scale. Subfigure (e) presents high-resolution TEM of a carbide with its corresponding FFT diffraction pattern (Z = [001]), confirming the FCC austenite matrix structure. Subfigure (f) shows an inverse FFT image near the carbide interface, with the magnified inset revealing lattice fringe distortions and edge dislocations (indicated by white arrows). Together, these images confirm the coexistence of an FCC austenite matrix, intragranular κ-carbide precipitates, and MC-type carbides (NbC-rich) in the 1Cu steel, where lattice distortions and edge dislocations near carbides generate high strain energy, serving as anodic sites that influence corrosion behavior.

Figure 13 presents comprehensive TEM-based characterization of the austenite matrix and carbides in Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-3Cu low-density steel. Subfigure (a) shows the austenitic matrix morphology. (b) displays selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of intracrystalline precipitates along the [001] zone axis, indexed with 002, 020, 001, and 011 reflections. (c) provides the dark-field image revealing the morphology of these nanoscale precipitates. (d) shows a larger carbide particle embedded in the matrix. (e) presents SAED patterns from the carbide, confirming it as NbC (MC-type) with an orientation relationship of [022]γ // [001]MC, showing 200, 111, 220, and 400 spots. (f) is an inverse FFT lattice image near the carbide, where terminating fringes indicate edge dislocations. (g) comprises EDS point spectroscopy and elemental mapping of the carbide region, demonstrating strong enrichment of Nb, C, Mo, and W within the carbide, and depletion of Fe, Cr, Al, and Cu. These results establish that the carbides are NbC particles that nucleate with Mo/W enrichment to reduce interfacial energy, while the associated edge dislocations and lattice distortion create high-energy anodic sites susceptible to localized corrosion.

The figure comprises eight panels combining TEM micrography and EDS elemental mapping to characterize Cu segregation at a grain boundary in Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel alloyed with Cu. The first panel is a TEM image (20 nm scale bar) showing a narrow, high-contrast dark feature aligned along a grain boundary, consistent with interfacial particle aggregation. The remaining seven panels are EDS surface-scan maps (each 20 nm scale bar) for Cu, Al, C, Cr, Fe, Nb, and Mo. The Cu map exhibits two yellow-boxed regions of intense orange signal, directly revealing localized Cu particle aggregation at the boundary. In marked contrast, the Al, C, Cr, Fe, Nb, and Mo maps show uniform green, purple, red, yellow, dark-blue, and cyan distributions, respectively, indicating these elements remain evenly dispersed in the matrix without detectable segregation. This elemental distribution pattern supports the conclusion that excess Cu in the 3Cu steel preferentially enriches austenite grain boundaries, providing the microstructural evidence for subsequent microgalvanic-driven grain-boundary corrosion.

This figure presents X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of corrosion products formed on Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–xCu (x = 0, 1, 3 wt%) low-density steels after 5 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Panels (a1)–(c1) display Fe 2p₃/₂ spectra deconvoluted into Fe₃O₄ (~709.96–710.34 eV), Fe₂O₃ (~711.16–711.46 eV), and FeOOH (~712.84–712.87 eV), with Fe₂O₃ being the most abundant iron oxide (43.54–44.15%). Panels (a2)–(c2) show Mn 2p₃/₂ spectra fitted with MnO (~640.85–641.37 eV) and MnO₂ (~642.41–643.10 eV); the MnO₂ proportion increases from 43.14% in 0Cu to 50.65% in 3Cu. Panels (a3)–(c3) confirm Al exists exclusively as Al₂O₃ near 74 eV. Panels (a4)–(c4) resolve O 1s into O²⁻, OH⁻, and C=O components, with OH⁻ dominating at 51.51–67.14%. Panels (b5) and (c5) reveal Cu 2p₃/₂ peaks for Cu₂O (~932.42–932.5 eV) and CuO (~935.13–935.2 eV) in the Cu-bearing steels. The data demonstrate that the surface film comprises a mixture of Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu oxides, supporting the conclusion that Cu addition alters the corrosion product chemistry and promotes higher Mn oxide enrichment, which accelerates corrosion kinetics.

This stacked horizontal bar chart quantifies the relative surface oxide composition, derived from XPS peak-area calculations, for Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C low-density steels containing 0 wt% Cu (0Cu), 1 wt% Cu (1Cu), and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu). Each bar is color-coded by phase—Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃, FeOOH, MnO, MnO₂, Al₂O₃, Cu₂O, and CuO—with percentages explicitly labeled. In 0Cu, Al₂O₃ is highest at 38.2%, MnO and MnO₂ together constitute 47%, and total Fe oxides are ~14.7%. In 1Cu, Mn oxides increase to ~57.2% (MnO 27.7%, MnO₂ 29.5%), Al₂O₃ decreases to 28.2%, and Cu₂O and CuO emerge at 1.2% and 1%, respectively. In 3Cu, Mn oxides dominate at ~77% (MnO 38%, MnO₂ 39%), Al₂O₃ drops sharply to 16.3%, Fe oxides fall to ~5.2%, and Cu oxides total ~1.5%. The data demonstrate that Cu addition progressively raises Mn oxide formation while diminishing protective Al₂O₃ and Fe oxide contents, consistent with the reported increase in corrosion sites from carbide refinement and grain-boundary Cu enrichment that accelerates localized corrosion.

Figure 17 presents high-resolution XPS spectra of stable passive films formed on Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steel with 0 Cu (panels a1–a4) and 3 Cu (panels b1–b5) after potentiostatic polarization at −0.3 V (vs. Ag–AgCl) in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 7200 s. Subfigures (a1) and (b1) show the Fe 2p3/2 region deconvoluted into Fe3O4 (712.80 eV, 32.51% and 713.10 eV, 32.87%), Fe2O3 (710.08 eV, 40.94% and 710.12 eV, 40.74%), and FeOOH (711.31 eV, 26.55% and 711.41 eV, 26.39%) for 0Cu and 3Cu, respectively. Subfigures (a2) and (b2) display Mn 2p3/2 spectra comprising MnO (640.68 eV, 54.11% and 640.72 eV, 63.84%) and MnO2 (642.59 eV, 45.89% and 642.58 eV, 36.16%). Al 2p spectra in (a3) and (b3) reveal Al2O3 (73.76 eV, 66.48% and 73.68 eV, 60.75%) and metallic Al (71.63 eV, 33.52% and 71.72 eV, 39.25%). O 1s spectra in (a4) and (b4) consist of C=O, OH−, and O2− components. Notably, (b5) for 3Cu shows Cu 2p3/2 peaks assigned to Cu2O (932.61 eV, 66.43%) and CuO (934.71 eV, 33.57%), which are absent in 0Cu. The 0Cu passive film exhibits a higher proportion of Al2O3 and more Fe/Al oxides overall, whereas 3Cu contains Cu oxides and an increased MnO fraction. These results indicate that Cu alloying introduces unstable Cu oxides into the passive film and reduces the protective Al-rich oxide proportion, consistent with the reported intensified microgalvanic corrosion and accelerated anodic dissolution in the 3Cu steel.

This stacked horizontal bar chart displays the oxide proportion of passivation films for Fe–30Mn–8Al–C-xCu steels, specifically the 0Cu and 3Cu samples labeled on the y-axis. As reported in the accompanying text, these films were formed under potentiostatic polarization at −0.3 V (vs. Ag–AgCl) in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 7200 s. The lower bar (0Cu) shows 4.8% Fe₃O₄, 7.4% Fe₂O₃, 5.9% FeOOH, 35.5% MnO, 30% MnO₂, 10.9% Al₂O₃, and 5.5% Al. The upper bar (3Cu) shows 4.3% Fe₃O₄, 6.7% Fe₂O₃, 5.4% FeOOH, 43.5% MnO, 24.6% MnO₂, 8.1% Al₂O₃, 5.3% Al, 1.1% Cu₂O, and 1% CuO. The chart indicates that 0Cu retains higher Fe and Al oxide contents, consistent with a more stable protective film, whereas 3Cu exhibits a higher Mn oxide fraction and contains Cu oxides. The related text interprets this contrast as evidence that Cu alloying promotes formation of an unstable oxide film and intensifies galvanic corrosion, thereby accelerating anodic dissolution and degrading passivation stability.

This figure presents a corrosion schematic diagram illustrating the degradation mechanisms of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C–xCu low-density steels (where x = 0, 1, and 3 wt%, labeled 0Cu, 1Cu, and 3Cu) during exposure to a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The left panels show cross-sectional views contrasting early- and late-stage corrosion processes, while the right panels display three-dimensional corroded surface morphologies. For 0Cu, carbides attached to the matrix surface create lattice distortion that initiates slight localized pitting. For 1Cu, Cu-induced refinement produces many fine carbides distributed across the surface, generating numerous local galvanic cells and a higher density of pits. For 3Cu, undissolved elemental Cu particles enriched at grain boundaries synergize with carbides to drive severe microgalvanic corrosion, resulting in pronounced intergranular attack. Below the schematics, reaction equations summarize the formation of FeOOH, Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄, Al₂O₃, MnO, MnO₂, and Cu₂O. The figure supports the conclusion that increasing Cu content progressively degrades corrosion resistance by shifting the dominant failure mode from isolated carbide-induced pitting to extensive Cu-carbide synergistic intergranular corrosion.
